hook: You already know that flipping a coin has 2 outcomes and rolling a die has 6 — but what happens when you combine multiple choices or arrange a group of people in a line? Without a systematic way to count, even simple probability questions become overwhelming guesswork. This unit gives you the tools to count possibilities quickly and confidently, unlocking the door to solving real probability problems.
concept: The unifying idea is that probability depends on counting — specifically, counting how many outcomes are possible and how many match what we care about. Every topic here builds a sharper counting tool, collectively forming the foundation of *combinatorics*.

topic_roadmap.description: You will learn to count the total number of outcomes for multi-step processes by multiplying the number of choices at each step, turning complex counting problems into simple arithmetic.
Master the distinction between ordered arrangements (permutations, $P(n,r)$) and unordered selections (combinations, $\binom{n}{r}$) so you can choose the right counting formula for any situation.
Apply extended counting techniques to handle scenarios where items can be reused or are identical, expanding your toolkit beyond the basic permutation and combination formulas.

Connect combinatorics directly to probability by using the Binomial Theorem to calculate the likelihood of exactly $k$ successes in repeated independent trials, such as flipping a coin or answering quiz questions.
